title: No Medical Life Insurance in Canada
seo_title: No Medical Life Insurance Canada | No Exam Policies | PolicyScanner
seo_description: Compare no medical life insurance in Canada. Get approved without a medical exam. Coverage for seniors, health conditions, and anyone declined before. Ontario advisors.

secondary_keywords: life insurance without medical exam ontario
_meta.conversion_goal: Lead capture — get declined, older, or health-impaired Canadians to compare no-medical options
_meta.page_role: Niche product page. Audience: seniors, people with health conditions, those previously declined. High-intent — they need coverage now.
_meta.writer_focus: Empathetic, validating tone. These readers may have been declined or feel embarrassed. Write with compassion: 'You can still get covered.' Be clear about trade-offs: lower coverage, higher premiums, waiting periods.

seo_keywords.density_rule: Use 'no medical life insurance' 2–3 times per 500 words. Also use 'guaranteed life insurance' and 'simplified issue' naturally.
synonyms: life insurance without a medical exam
natural_variations: life insurance with no health questions
guaranteed approval life insurance in Canada
seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Grace, 67, from Hamilton — type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure, previously declined twice. Got $50,000 guaranteed issue whole life for $148/month through PolicyScanner.
seo_keywords.avoid: Do not imply these policies are the same quality as fully underwritten coverage. Be honest: they cost more and cover less. Readers will discover this anyway — be upfront.

page_data.heading: No Medical Life Insurance: You Can Still Get Covered
page_data.subheading: Previously declined? Have a health condition? Over 65? No medical exam life insurance in Canada gives you guaranteed or simplified coverage — no doctor, no blood tests.

_writer_notes.focus: Inclusive, reassuring tone. This audience has often been rejected. The hero message is: 'You can still get covered.' Avoid clinical language.
key_points: No blood tests, no medical exam, no doctor visit
Two types: Simplified Issue (health questions) and Guaranteed Issue (no questions at all)
Approval can be same-day

page_data.heading: Two Types of No Medical Life Insurance

_writer_notes.focus: Clearly distinguish Simplified Issue vs. Guaranteed Issue. Most readers don't know the difference.
key_points: Simplified Issue: no medical exam but has health questions (usually 5–15). Some conditions may disqualify. Coverage up to $500K. Lower premiums than GI.
Guaranteed Issue: NO health questions, NO exam, CANNOT be declined. Usually for ages 40–85. Coverage limited to $10K–$50K. Higher premiums. 2-year graded benefit period.
Which is better? Simplified issue if you can qualify — it costs less and covers more. Guaranteed issue is the last resort.
_writer_notes.structure: Two columns or two clear subsections. Comparison table at the end of the section.

page_data.heading: What Is the Graded Benefit Period?

_writer_notes.focus: Critical concept to explain honestly. Most guaranteed issue policies have a 2-year waiting period. If you die of illness in year 1–2, your family gets premiums back + interest, NOT the full benefit.
key_points: Usually 2 years from policy start date
If you die within 2 years from illness: insurer returns premiums paid + 10% interest to beneficiary
If you die within 2 years from accident: full benefit usually paid immediately
After 2 years: full benefit paid for any cause of death
Why? Insurer is taking on unknown health risk — the waiting period prevents anti-selection
_writer_notes.structure: Explain clearly, then example: 'If Grace gets her $50K policy in January and passes away in September from a heart condition, her daughter receives $1,776 in returned premiums — not $50,000. But after 2 years, the full $50,000 would be paid.' Be direct.

page_data.heading: Who Should Consider No Medical Life Insurance?

_writer_notes.focus: Help readers self-identify. Four specific groups.
key_points: Seniors 60+: healthy underwriting standards get stricter with age — no-medical fills the gap
People with chronic conditions: diabetes, heart disease, cancer history, COPD, HIV — may be declined for fully underwritten policies
Previously declined: if you've been declined once, no-medical may be your only option
Those wanting fast approval: no-medical is approved in hours or days vs. weeks for fully underwritten
_writer_notes.callout_after: Contextual note: 'If you're under 50 and in reasonable health, you should first apply for a fully underwritten policy — it will cost less and cover more. Use no-medical insurance only if needed.'

page_data.heading: How Much Does No Medical Life Insurance Cost?

_writer_notes.focus: Real premium estimates. Show that no-medical costs more than traditional. Be honest — readers will compare.
key_points: Simplified Issue, $100K whole life, age 55, male, non-smoker: ~$120–$160/month
Simplified Issue, $100K whole life, age 60, female, non-smoker: ~$130–$170/month
Guaranteed Issue, $25K whole life, age 65, male: ~$80–$110/month
Guaranteed Issue, $50K whole life, age 65, female: ~$120–$160/month
Compare: $100K fully underwritten term, age 55 is often $50–$80/month — so no-medical costs 2–3x more per dollar of coverage
_writer_notes.structure: Table: Type / Coverage / Age / Monthly Estimate. Add honest note at bottom: 'No-medical insurance costs more. But if you can't qualify for standard coverage, it may be your best option.'

page_data.heading: Top No Medical Life Insurance Providers in Canada

_writer_notes.focus: Brief overview of main providers specializing in no-medical. Don't rank or endorse — just describe what they're known for.
key_points: Canada Protection Plan: largest no-medical insurer in Canada — wide coverage range
Assumption Life: good simplified issue options, strong Catholic roots (not relevant — just their reputation)
Foresters Financial: competitive guaranteed issue with fraternal benefits
Industrial Alliance (iA): simplified issue with competitive rates for healthy seniors
Empire Life: strong term life alternative for those who can qualify medically
_writer_notes.callout_after: PolicyScanner compares multiple no-medical providers — get your quote in 2 minutes.

page_data.heading: Simplified Issue vs. Guaranteed Issue: Quick Comparison

_writer_notes.focus: Quick reference table. Six rows comparing the two types on key dimensions.
key_points: Health questions: Yes (SI) vs. No (GI)
Maximum coverage: Up to $500K (SI) vs. Up to $50K (GI)
Graded period: Sometimes (SI) vs. Usually 2 years (GI)
Monthly cost: Moderate (SI) vs. High per dollar of coverage (GI)
Best for: Health conditions but not terminal (SI) vs. Any health history, final expenses (GI)
Approval time: 1–3 days (SI) vs. Same day (GI)

page_data.heading: No Medical Life Insurance — Frequently Asked Questions

faqs.question: Can I get no medical life insurance over age 70 in Canada?
faqs.answer: Yes. Guaranteed issue policies are typically available to Canadians aged 40–85, depending on the insurer. Coverage limits are lower at older ages (often $10,000–$25,000), and premiums are higher. Most people over 70 use these policies to cover funeral costs and final debts.
faqs._guidance: Clear answer. Mention typical maximum age and coverage limits honestly.

faqs.question: Is no medical life insurance worth it?
faqs.answer: It depends on your situation. If you cannot qualify for a standard fully-underwritten policy due to health or age, then yes — having some coverage is better than none. If you are in reasonable health, a fully underwritten policy will almost always provide better coverage at a lower price.
faqs._guidance: Balanced, honest answer. Don't oversell no-medical — help readers make the right choice.

faqs.question: What if I only need enough to cover my funeral?
faqs.answer: A guaranteed issue policy of $15,000–$25,000 is commonly used as final expense insurance in Canada. It covers funeral costs, which average $8,000–$15,000 in Ontario, and leaves a small amount for other end-of-life expenses. Foresters and Canada Protection Plan are popular choices for this use case.
faqs._guidance: Validate the use case. Don't be judgmental about small face amounts — many Canadians just want to cover their funeral.

faqs.question: Can I be declined for guaranteed issue life insurance?
faqs.answer: No. Guaranteed issue life insurance cannot decline you for any health reason. As long as you meet the age requirements (usually 40–80 or 40–85 depending on insurer) and are a Canadian resident, you are approved.
faqs._guidance: This is the key selling feature. Be definitive.

faqs.question: Do no medical insurance companies check my health records?
faqs.answer: For simplified issue: they ask health questions and may verify answers with MIB (Medical Information Bureau) or prescription drug database. For guaranteed issue: no health check of any kind. The 2-year graded benefit period is the insurer's protection instead.
faqs._guidance: Explain how each type handles verification. MIB is a real credentialing body — mention it.
